section.title: The Nations Have Defiled Your Temple
section.sectionRationale: An Asaphite communal lament over Jerusalem's destruction, most naturally read against the Babylonian ruin of the city and temple in 586 BC. It moves from raw grief over defiled holy ground and unburied dead to a plea for God's own name and a vow of unending praise.
section.thesis: When the nations defile God's holy place and mock His people, His covenant name, not Israel's own merit, is the ground on which His people plead for atonement, deliverance, and the reversal of their reproach.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the ruin and reproach, 1-4; the plea for God's anger to fall on the nations instead, 5-7; the appeal for mercy and atonement for the sake of His name, 8-9; and the call for justice answered by a vow of everlasting praise, 10-13.

verses.text: The nations, O God, have invaded Your {{nachalah|inheritance}}; they have defiled Your holy temple and reduced Jerusalem to rubble.
They have given the corpses of Your servants as food to the birds of the air, the flesh of Your saints to the beasts of the earth.
They have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem, and there is no one to bury the dead.
We have become a reproach to our neighbors, a scorn and derision to those around us.

units.label: How long, O LORD
units.note: plea for God's anger to turn to the nations

verses.text: How long, O LORD? Will You be angry forever? Will Your jealousy burn like fire?
Pour out Your wrath on the nations that do not acknowledge You, on the kingdoms that refuse to call on Your name,
for they have devoured Jacob and devastated his homeland.

units.label: For the sake of Your name
units.note: atonement pleaded on God's own reputation

verses.text: Do not hold past sins against us; let Your compassion come quickly, for we are brought low.
Help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of Your name; deliver us and {{kipper|atone}} for our sins, for the sake of Your name.
Why should the nations ask, "Where is their God?" Before our eyes, make known among the nations Your vengeance for the bloodshed of Your servants.
May the groans of the captives reach You; by the strength of Your arm preserve those condemned to death.
Pay back into the laps of our neighbors sevenfold the reproach they hurled at You, O Lord.
Then we Your people, the {{tson|sheep}} of Your pasture, will thank You forever; from generation to generation we will declare Your praise.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, a possession held by right of covenant. Israel and the temple are God's own nachalah, so the invasion of verse 1 is not merely a political disaster but an assault on what belongs to God Himself.

kipper.gloss: To atone, cover, or make propitiation. The plea 'atone for our sins' asks God to provide the covering for guilt Himself, for His own name's sake, anticipating the atonement finally accomplished in Christ.

tson.gloss: Flock or sheep. Even after describing slaughter and ruin, the psalm's closing vow calls Israel 'the sheep of Your pasture,' the same shepherd imagery that runs through this cluster of psalms and finds its fulfillment in Christ, the Good Shepherd.

basic.context: This communal lament mourns Jerusalem's temple defiled, its walls in rubble, and its dead left unburied, most likely written after the Babylonian destruction in 586 BC. Rather than despairing, Asaph turns grief into prayer, asking God to act for His own name's sake and promising unending praise once He does.
basic.hermeneutics: This is honest, Spirit-given lament and imprecation, appealing to God's own justice and reputation rather than to Israel's merit. Its plea for vengeance is not personal vindictiveness but a cry that God's holy name be vindicated before the watching nations.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is God's nachalah, His own inheritance, so its ruin is an assault on what is His. The psalm asks Him to kipper, atone, for their sins, a covering God alone can provide. Even in ruin, the closing verse calls the people tson, sheep, held safe in God's own pasture.
basic.typology: The plea to 'atone for our sins, for the sake of Your name' anticipates the atonement God Himself provides in Christ, who satisfies both justice and mercy without any merit on the sinner's part. The closing image of God's flock anticipates the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep.
basic.christ: The psalm's cry for atonement grounded in God's name alone, not Israel's worthiness, finds its answer in the cross, where God Himself provides the atoning sacrifice, Romans 3:25-26. The closing confession of the people as sheep of His pasture points to Jesus, the Good Shepherd, John 10:11.
basic.god: God is shown as the rightful owner of His inheritance, whose holy name is at stake in His people's suffering, and as a God whose compassion can be pleaded even after real, admitted sin, because deliverance rests on His own glory rather than human merit.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:25-26 shows God providing atonement Himself through Christ, for the sake of His own righteousness and name. John 10:11 identifies Jesus as the Good Shepherd of the flock this psalm calls sheep of God's pasture. Revelation 6:9-11 echoes this psalm's cry for the vindication of martyred blood.
basic.oneStory: This lament over a defiled temple and a devastated people pleads for God to act for His own name, not for Israel's merit, a pattern of grace that reaches its fullest answer in Christ, who atones for sin so that God's flock may praise Him forever.

context.literary: A four-part communal lament: ruin and reproach, 1-4; petition that God's anger fall on the nations, 5-7; appeal for mercy and atonement, 8-9; and a closing plea for justice sealed by a vow of praise, 10-13.
context.historical: Most conservative readings tie this psalm to the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem and the temple in 586 BC, 2 Kings 25, when the sanctuary was defiled, the city burned, and the dead left exposed exactly as described here.
context.canonical: A companion to Psalm 74 and Psalm 137 in mourning the ruin of Zion, and part of the Asaphite collection that both laments national disaster and presses God's people to hope in His name rather than their own standing.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice the community's raw grief over Jerusalem's desecration honestly before God, to plead for deliverance grounded in God's own name and compassion rather than Israel's merit, and to call for justice on those who mocked God's people and His reputation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical catastrophe; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that God's people may lament honestly, confess sin corporately, and appeal to God's own glory as the ground of hope, rather than pretending unbroken confidence or hiding real anguish.

debates.question: Is the request in verse 12 to 'pay back sevenfold' a call for personal vengeance or a plea for God's own justice?

views.label: A prayer that God Himself, not the psalmist, execute justice, consistent with leaving vengeance to God
views.case: The whole psalm is addressed to God as the one being asked to act; the psalmist takes no vengeance personally, echoing the pattern later stated explicitly in Romans 12:19.

views.label: An imprecation reflecting the ancient Near Eastern legal principle of proportionate, even multiplied, retribution for covenant violation
views.case: The sevenfold measure echoes Leviticus 26:18's language of complete recompense, framing the request as a plea for God's full covenant justice rather than an arbitrary demand.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the prayer entrusts vengeance entirely to God rather than to human hands, honoring His justice while voicing the real anguish of the wronged.

study.originalLanguages: The devastated land and people are called God's nachalah, His own inheritance, so their ruin is an affront to Him directly. The plea to kipper, atone, asks God to cover the guilt Himself, not on the ground of merit but for His name's sake. Even in the ashes, the psalm closes by naming the people tson, sheep, held in God's pasture.

typology.type: Atonement pleaded for God's name's sake alone, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: The atonement God provides in Christ, who satisfies divine justice and mercy together, apart from human merit, Romans 3:25-26.

typology.type: The people as sheep of God's pasture, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, the Good Shepherd, who lays down His life for the sheep, John 10:11.

study.christ: This psalm's plea, 'atone for our sins, for the sake of Your name,' finds its answer at the cross, where God Himself provides the atoning sacrifice through Christ so that His righteousness and mercy meet without compromise, Romans 3:25-26. The closing confession of the people as the sheep of God's pasture anticipates Jesus, the Good Shepherd who gathers and keeps His flock, John 10:11, and whose resurrection answers the taunting question of the nations, 'Where is their God?', Acts 2:24, 32.
study.god: God is the rightful owner of His temple and people, whose holy name is bound up with their fate before the watching nations. He hears honest lament, receives confession of past sins, and can be pleaded with on the ground of His own glory and compassion rather than any human merit.

crossReferences.note: God Himself provides the atonement in Christ, satisfying His justice and mercy for the sake of His own name.
Jesus as the Good Shepherd fulfills the psalm's closing picture of God's people as sheep of His pasture.
Echoes this psalm's cry for the vindication of the blood of God's servants.

study.oneStory: Psalm 79 turns the ashes of a defiled temple into a prayer for God to act for His own name, not for Israel's merit. That same pattern, atonement grounded in God's glory rather than human worthiness, reaches its full answer in Christ, who atones for sin so that God's flock may praise Him from generation to generation.

sermon.title: How Much Does God Love This Church?
sermon.why: Piper preaches from Psalm 79, including its appeal to God's name in verse 9, to show how God's own love and glory, not His people's merit, ground His care for a suffering congregation.

quiz.q: What has happened to Jerusalem and the temple according to verses 1-3?

options.text: The nations have invaded, defiled the holy temple, reduced the city to rubble, and left the dead unburied.
options.note: This is the exact devastation described in the opening verses.

options.text: The city has flourished under a period of peace.
options.note: The opposite is true; the psalm opens with ruin and desecration.

options.text: The temple has been rebuilt more gloriously than before.
options.note: The psalm describes destruction, not rebuilding.

options.text: The people have voluntarily abandoned the city.
options.note: The devastation comes from invasion, not voluntary abandonment.

quiz.q: On what basis does the psalmist ask God to help and deliver, in verse 9?

options.text: For the glory of God's own name, not for any merit of the people.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly grounds the plea in God's name and glory.

options.text: Because Israel had kept the law perfectly.
options.note: The psalm openly confesses past sins rather than claiming perfect obedience.

options.text: Because the surrounding nations deserved punishment more than Israel.
options.note: Comparative merit is not the stated basis for the plea.

options.text: Because the people had suffered enough to earn deliverance.
options.note: Suffering is described, but the explicit ground given is God's name, not accumulated merit through suffering.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to do regarding the nations, in verse 6?

options.text: Pour out His wrath on the nations that do not acknowledge Him.
options.note: Verse 6 makes this request directly.

options.text: Bless the nations regardless of their actions.
options.note: The request is for God's wrath, not an unconditional blessing.

options.text: Ignore the nations entirely and focus only on Israel.
options.note: The nations are directly addressed, not ignored.

options.text: Make peace with the nations without addressing their sin.
options.note: The plea calls for justice on the nations' unacknowledged wrongdoing, not simple peace.

quiz.q: What vow does the psalm make once God acts, in verse 13?

options.text: God's people, the sheep of His pasture, will thank Him forever, from generation to generation.
options.note: This is the closing vow of the psalm.

options.text: The people will never trust God again after such suffering.
options.note: The psalm ends in a vow of praise, not lasting distrust.

options.text: The people will seek out other gods for greater protection.
options.note: The psalm's confidence remains fixed on the LORD alone.

options.text: The people will praise God only until the next crisis arises.
options.note: The vow is for perpetual, generational praise, not a temporary response.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's plea for atonement point toward the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the atonement God provides in Christ, for His own name's sake rather than human merit.
options.note: Romans 3:25-26 shows God Himself providing atonement through Christ on this same basis.

options.text: It has no connection to New Testament teaching on atonement.
options.note: The plea for atonement grounded in God's name directly anticipates the gospel's logic.

options.text: It teaches that atonement must always be earned through suffering.
options.note: The psalm explicitly asks for atonement as a gift for God's name's sake, not as something earned.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of the temple's rebuilding.
options.note: The psalm addresses atonement and deliverance, not a specific rebuilding timeline.

questions: The psalmist names the devastation honestly before God rather than minimizing it, verses 1-4. What pain or loss are you tempted to minimize instead of bringing honestly to God in prayer?
The plea in verse 9 rests entirely on God's own name and glory, not on Israel's merit. Where are you trying to earn God's help instead of resting on His character and promises in Christ?
The psalm asks God to redirect His righteous anger toward those who reject Him, verses 5-7. How do you hold together honest anger at injustice with trust in God's own timing and justice?

Even after admitting 'past sins,' the psalmist asks for God's compassion to 'come quickly,' verse 8. What sin do you need to confess honestly today, trusting that God's compassion, not your track record, is your hope?
The psalm ends with a vow to praise God forever once He acts, verse 13. What would it look like for you to vow praise now, even before your circumstances change?

translationNotes.note: 'Inheritance' (nachalah) frames the ruin of Jerusalem and the temple not merely as a national tragedy but as an affront to what belongs to God Himself.
'Atone' (kipper) asks God to provide the covering for sin Himself, on the ground of His own name and glory rather than the people's merit.
'The sheep of Your pasture' (tson) closes the psalm with covenant shepherd imagery that recurs across this cluster of psalms and finds its fulfillment in Christ the Good Shepherd.
